---
title: "Unix/Linux and the command line"
author: "Gary Napier gary.napier@ccn.ac.uk"
format:
  revealjs:
    smaller: true
    logo: img/ccn_logo.png
    css: ../quarto/slides_styles.css
    footer: "Access to Computing - Unix/Linux and command line"
    embed-resources: true
---

# Command line basics

## Command line basics

- What is the command line?
- Basically, every time you do anything on a computer, like double click on a file to open it, delete a file or start an application, something is happening "in the background".
- In other words, some code is being executed. 
- The command line is just a way of by-passing all the point-and-click/drag-and-drop, and instead running those commands directly with code. 
- This means you can program the computer to execute a set of instructions. 
- Plus it's very powerful as there are loads of commands and options, and you can hack the system to your liking and just interact with it in a very flexible way. 

## Command line basics

‼️DANGER‼️️

- The command line is very powerful, especially the command `rm` ("remove")
  - This will delete files without asking if you're sure, and there's no way to retrieve it (no Recycle bin!)
  - It's even possible to erase your entire machine with one line of `rm` code and certain arguments to the command. 

## Command line basics

- Open a terminal, should see:

```
<username>$

```


## Command line basics

Try a few simple commands:

```
ls
ls -a
ls -a -l
ls -a -l -h
ls -alh
ls -alh Documents
echo "hello"
cd Documents
cd ../

```
## Command line basics

Meaning:

|Command | Meaning |
| ls | List files |
| ls -a | List all files (including hidden) |
| ls -a -l | List all files and put them in a neat list, displaying properties |
| ls -a -l -h | List all files and put them in a neat list, displaying properties, and make human readable |
| ls -alh  | List all files and put them in a neat list, displaying properties |
| ls -alh Documents |  | List all files and put them in a neat list, displaying properties, in the Docuemtns folder |
| echo "hello" | Print the string "Hello" |
| cd Documents | Change directory to Documents |
| cd ../ | Change directory to one level up |

## Command line basics

Parts of a command:

| Command | Flag (option) | Argument |
| ls | | |
| ls | -a |  |
| ls | -a -l | | 
| ls | -a -l -h | | 
| ls | -alh | |
| ls | -alh | Documents | 
| Do this... | in this way... | to this object |

## Command line basics

Other basic commands:

| Command | Meaning |
| cp [file] [dir] | copy |
| mv [file] [dir] | move (or rename) |
| rm [file] | remove |
| pwd | Print Working Directory |
| mkdir [dir name] | Make new Directory |
| cat [file] | print the contents of a file |
| less [file] | print contents of a file (but allows scrolling; press q to escape) |

## Command line basics

Navigation

- Knowing where you are is key to using the command line.
- Run `cd ~/` to navigate to the home directory
- Run `pwd` (Print Working Directory) to see where you are. 
  - Should see `/c/Users/<username>`
- Run `ls Documents` to see what's in there. 
- Now `cd` into Documents. 
- Run `mkdir test_dir` (never spaces, remember! Now you can see why we don't use spaces)
- `cd test_dir`
- Now try `ls Documents` again
- Should see `ls: cannot access 'Documents': No such file or directory`
- This is because the file arguments to the commands are *relative* to the current working directory

## Tips 

- Tab-completion
